# Obesity & metabolic syndrome

> A patient with a "reassuring" BMI can still be carrying the fat that matters. Obesity and the
> metabolic syndrome are a pattern written by biology and environment together, not a number on a
> scale or a failure of willpower. The clinical work is to recognise the phenotype, understand why a
> cluster of risks travels as a group, and start lifestyle-first care, knowing what to refer onward
> and doing all of it without stigma.

## Obesity & metabolic syndrome at a glance

Over- and under-nutrition are the two tails of malnutrition; this part opens with one tail and closes
with the other (the *Undernutrition & Malnutrition* chapter). Obesity is the more familiar tail, and
it is the entry point to a cascade the next chapters follow, through the *Type 2 Diabetes* and the
*Cardiovascular Disease & Hypertension* chapters, because the same central, insulin-resistant fat that
defines obesity is much of what drives those diseases.

The aim at this stage is deliberately bounded: **recognise and classify** over-nutrition, **explain**
why its risks cluster, and begin **first-line lifestyle care**, then **refer** for the specialist
tiers. Three commitments run through the chapter:

- **Singapore acts at lower numbers than the global cut-offs**, for a real physiological reason.
- **Diet quality is the lever**, more than calorie arithmetic alone, and sleep and stress are genuine levers too.
- **Framing is clinical, not cosmetic**: weight-inclusive, person-first language is part of the care.

The physiology underneath, how energy balance is measured and how appetite is set by leptin, ghrelin
and the hypothalamus, is the *Energy Balance & Body Composition* and *Appetite & Weight Regulation*
chapters' territory; this chapter stands on it rather than repeating it.

## Recognising the phenotype: BMI, waist, and why Singapore acts earlier

BMI is a **screening proxy**, not a measurement of fat. It cannot separate muscle from fat or say
*where* fat sits, and that matters here, because Singaporean adults carry **more body fat, and
proportionally more visceral fat, at a given BMI** than the Western populations the WHO cut-offs were
built on. The local Yishun Study made the gap concrete: matched for BMI, age and sex, Singaporeans had
higher percentage body fat, and classifying by body fat rather than BMI flagged far more adults as
over-fat than BMI did [@chen2021yishun]. Because Asians reach diabetes and cardiovascular risk at a
lower BMI, Singapore shifts its action points **down** from the global figures [@whoExpert2004AsianBMI].

So there are, in effect, **two number-lines**:

- **WHO (global):** overweight ≥25, obese ≥30 kg/m².
- **Singapore (clinical action):** **intervene at ≥23**, treat as **high-risk at ≥27.5** kg/m² [@hpbmoh2016obesityCPG; @sgObesityBMI].

The single most useful bedside add-on is the **waist circumference**, because it reads *central* fat,
the metabolically active kind. Here too the Asian cut-offs sit lower: **≥90 cm in men and ≥80 cm in
women** [@alberti2009harmonizingMetS; @sgObesityBMI]. A patient with a "reassuring" BMI of 24 and a
waist over the cut-off is still carrying risk; BMI and waist are read **together**, never BMI alone.

## The metabolic syndrome: a cluster, not a disease

Central fat rarely travels alone. The **metabolic syndrome** names the tendency for five
cardiometabolic problems to appear together, and the harmonised international definition is simply
**any three of the following five**, with **no single obligatory component** [@alberti2009harmonizingMetS]:

- **Central obesity** — waist ≥90 cm (men) / ≥80 cm (women) for Asian populations;
- **Raised triglycerides** — ≥1.7 mmol/L;
- **Low HDL-cholesterol** — <1.0 mmol/L (men) / <1.3 mmol/L (women);
- **Raised blood pressure** — ≥130/85 mmHg;
- **Raised fasting glucose** — ≥5.6 mmol/L;

(with "on treatment for this" counting the same as a raised reading).

What ties them together is **insulin resistance and central adiposity** at the hub, which is why the
five cluster rather than scatter, and why the syndrome is best understood as a **risk marker** that
flags markedly raised risk of type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease rather than a single disease
with a single drug [@kaurMetsDefinitionsReview2014; @metsDefinitionsControversies2011]. The label is a
prompt to look at the whole cluster and its shared root, not to treat "the syndrome" as an entity.
Local data add a further nuance: the components do not always behave as one tidy syndrome across
Singapore's ethnic groups [@angMetsSingaporeNHS2005].

The interactive below puts the hub-and-spokes picture together: toggle a patient's findings and watch
the three-of-five threshold light up, and see how sleep loss and stress feed the same central hub.

<div data-island="mets-cluster" data-src="/diagrams/mets-cluster.yml"></div>

## The science underneath: how obesity produces the cluster

Why do those five problems travel together? Because **body fat is an active endocrine organ, not inert
padding**, and *where* it sits and *how* it behaves is what links a waistline to a blood-sugar reading.
The normal mechanics of fat storage and insulin action belong to the *Energy Balance & Body
Composition* and *Integrative Metabolism* chapters; here we follow how they go wrong.

![How obesity produces the metabolic syndrome — a chronic energy surplus enlarges visceral and ectopic fat, which becomes inflamed and shifts its adipokine output, driving insulin resistance (the shared hub) and blunting satiety, so the five-component cluster emerges from one mechanism.](/figures/mechanisms/obesity-pathophysiology.svg){#fig-obesity-pathophysiology width="88%" fig-align="center"}

**It starts with *where* the fat is.** A chronic energy surplus enlarges fat cells, but the harmful
growth is in **visceral and ectopic fat**, packed around the abdominal organs and deposited in the
liver and muscle rather than under the skin. Visceral fat drains straight to the liver and behaves
quite differently from subcutaneous fat: it is an *independent* marker of cardiometabolic risk, over
and above BMI [@neeland2019visceral]. That is why the waist circumference, a bedside read of central
fat, adds so much to the BMI.

**Enlarged fat becomes inflamed fat.** As fat cells hypertrophy and outgrow their blood supply, the
tissue becomes stressed and recruits immune cells (macrophages), settling into a state of **chronic
low-grade inflammation**. This adipose inflammation is now understood to *cause* insulin resistance
rather than merely accompany it [@hotamisligil2006inflammation]. Its hormonal output shifts too: the
**adipokine profile** changes, with insulin-sensitising, anti-inflammatory **adiponectin falling**
while pro-inflammatory signals (TNF-α, IL-6, resistin) **rise** [@ouchi2011adipokines].

**The result is insulin resistance, the hub.** Add a spillover of free fatty acids and ectopic lipid
("lipotoxicity") that jams insulin signalling in the liver and muscle [@samuel2012mechanisms], and the
body's cells turn **less responsive to insulin**. Insulin resistance is the shared root the cluster
points back to: it pushes blood glucose up, drives the liver to over-produce triglyceride (raising
triglycerides, lowering HDL), and contributes to higher blood pressure. The five components emerge from
one mechanism.

**And appetite is pulled the wrong way.** Fat also signals the brain. In obesity **leptin**, the
satiety signal from fat introduced in the *Appetite & Weight Regulation* chapter, runs *high*, but the
brain stops listening: central leptin signalling is blunted, a **leptin resistance** that weakens
satiety and makes the surplus harder to reverse [@myers2010leptin]. The same biology that builds the
cluster also *defends* the weight, which is why lifestyle change is a genuine physiological lever rather
than a test of willpower.

## Why weight accumulates: energy balance, but quality is the lever

At the level of physics, weight gain is a **chronic positive energy balance**: small daily surpluses
banked over years. But "eat less, move more" is a weak clinical lever on its own, because it treats
every calorie as equal. *What* is eaten shapes how much gets eaten. Two drivers do most of the work.

**Ultra-processed foods drive over-eating even when the nutrients are matched.** In a controlled
inpatient trial, people freely eating an ultra-processed diet took in about **500 kcal a day more**,
and gained weight, compared with the same people on a matched unprocessed diet, despite the meals being
equated for sugar, fat, fibre and salt [@hall2019upf]. Higher ultra-processed intake tracks with
obesity and, more convincingly, with incident diabetes and cardiovascular outcomes across large
populations [@lane2024upfumbrella].

**Sugar-sweetened drinks add calories the body barely notices.** Liquid sugar is poorly compensated at
the next meal, and higher intake produces dose-dependent weight gain [@nguyen2023ssb]. Swapping to a
"diet" version is not the fix the label implies: the WHO advises **against relying on non-sugar
sweeteners** for weight control; water and unsweetened drinks plus habit change are the answer
[@who2023nss].

The targets are simple to state (free sugars under 10% of energy, ideally under 5%, saturated fat under
10%, *trans* fat under 1%, salt under 5 g a day [@who2020diet; @who2015sugars]) and Singapore
translates them into tools that need no arithmetic: **My Healthy Plate** (quarter wholegrains, quarter
protein, half fruit and vegetables) and **Nutri-Grade** drink grades A–D [@hpbHealthyPlate; @hpbNutriGrade].
The message is **patterns over single nutrients**, minimally processed over ultra-processed [@aha2021diet].

::: {.callout-note}
## 🔬 Food science: why "same nutrients" still means more calories
Ultra-processing does more than change a label. Soft, energy-dense, quick-to-eat foods are consumed
**faster and in greater volume** before fullness signals catch up, so intake runs ahead of need even
when a diet is matched gram-for-gram on sugar, fat and fibre. That is the finding from the inpatient
feeding trial that makes processing, not just composition, a lever worth naming [@hall2019upf].
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out
The hawker plate is where most of this is decided. Energy-dense staples add up fast: a plate of char
kway teow runs to roughly **744 kcal with about 38 g of fat**, close to a whole day's fat in one dish
[@healthhubHawkerSwaps]. Lower-density swaps that still fit My Healthy Plate: **thunder tea rice,
non-fried yong tau foo** (more vegetables and tofu, clear soup), **sliced fish bee hoon soup**, or
**teochew porridge** with lean sides. For drinks, read the **Nutri-Grade**: choose grade A/B, or go
*kopi/teh-o kosong* or plain water instead of a grade C/D condensed-milk or bubble-tea order.
:::

![Char kway teow — a Singapore hawker favourite and a useful energy-density anchor: roughly 744 kcal and 38 g of fat in one plate.](/figures/food/char-kway-teow.jpg){#fig-char-kway-teow width="70%" fig-align="center"}

## Managing over-nutrition: lifestyle first, then refer

The reassuring headline is that the target is **modest**. A sustained **5–10% weight loss** yields a
disproportionate, dose-dependent cardiometabolic payoff, improving glucose, blood pressure and lipids
well out of proportion to the number on the scale [@wing2011modest]. Lifestyle change is the
**cornerstone**; everything else is added on top of it, not instead of it.

Five levers make up first-line care, and two of them are routinely under-used:

- **Diet pattern** — pattern-level substitutions and My Healthy Plate, aiming for 5–10% loss over 6–12 months, not a branded diet [@aceBmiControl2026].
- **Physical activity** — which works *partly independently of the scale*: it cuts visceral fat, improves insulin sensitivity and preserves lean mass even when weight barely moves [@kolnes2021exercisefat]. Diet quality remains the larger *weight* lever, so pair the two.
- **Sleep** — a genuine, modifiable weight lever. Short or poor sleep measurably **raises energy intake** (with no matching rise in expenditure), and *extending* sleep pulls intake back down [@alkhatib2017; @tasali2022sleepextension]; short habitual sleep tracks with more central obesity [@kohanmoo2024shortsleep]. Teach the appetite-hormone story **directionally**: the classic leptin-down, ghrelin-up account is echoed observationally, but a 2025 meta-analysis of trials found no reliable hormone shift [@spiegel2004; @sleepHormonesMeta2025]; the robust, teachable signal is **intake**.
- **Stress** — chronic stress and cortisol bias eating toward energy-dense "comfort" food and channel fat to the abdomen, a self-reinforcing loop that runs straight to the central-adiposity hub [@dallman2003]. (Mindfulness programmes for weight are still **exploratory**: name the mechanism, not a guaranteed fix [@daubenmier2011].)
- **Behaviour change** — the skill that makes the other four stick, developed in the *Behaviour-Change Counselling* chapter.

::: {.callout-note}
## The treatment ladder — and where recognition stops
Above lifestyle sit two specialist tiers: **anti-obesity medication** (including the newer GLP-1
receptor agonists) and **metabolic-bariatric surgery**, which can achieve substantially greater loss
for selected patients [@lee2023obesitylandscape]. The job at this stage is to **recognise that these
tiers exist and refer**, not to prescribe or select them. Two evidence cautions travel with the
prevention story. First, the landmark trials that show lifestyle can prevent or even remit diabetes did
so under **intensive, structured programmes** (the Diabetes Prevention Program was a 16-session
curriculum; DiRECT's ~46% remission applied to *early, non-insulin-treated* patients and is untested in
Asian primary care) [@dpp2002; @lean2018direct]. Second, improving those risk factors is **not** the
same as preventing heart attacks: an intensive lifestyle programme in established diabetes improved
markers but did **not** reduce cardiovascular events [@lookahead2013]. The mechanics of remission
belong to the *Type 2 Diabetes* chapter.
:::

## Weight-inclusive care: the framing is part of the treatment

How the conversation is framed changes its outcome, so this is a clinical skill, not a courtesy.
**Weight stigma is a health hazard**: it degrades the quality of care, drives patients to avoid
appointments, and, by raising cortisol and comfort-eating, can *paradoxically promote weight gain*, the
loop captured by the "COBWEBS" model [@tomiyama2014cobwebs; @phelan2015impact; @puhl2023barriers].
International consensus now condemns weight stigma outright and asks clinicians to switch focus from the
scale to health and behaviour [@rubino2020consensus; @tylka2014weightinclusive; @batterham2020switchingfocus].
This is a live local issue: in a Singapore survey most respondents had felt weight-stigmatised, and a
fifth felt disrespected or discouraged by a healthcare worker [@chue2022stigmasg; @goff2023sgweightbias].

::: {.callout-important}
## The language shift
Use **person-first language** ("a person with obesity", not "an obese patient"), **ask permission**
before discussing weight, and set goals around **health behaviours and metabolic markers** rather than
the scale alone, while still holding the ~5–10% target that earns the metabolic payoff
[@weghuber2023peoplefirst; @batterham2020switchingfocus]. Shaming does not motivate; it backfires.
:::

## The Singapore picture

Every thread above has a sharp local form.

**Two number-lines, one message.** Singapore reports *population* obesity at the WHO ≥30 threshold,
which rose from **10.5% to 12.7%** between 2019–20 and 2023–24, for international comparability, but
*clinical* action starts far earlier, and the ≥27.5 high-risk band is a stable **roughly one in five
adults** [@mohNPHS2024]. "12.7% obese" is the newspaper figure; **≥27.5 / ≥23** is the clinic figure.
Tellingly, national physical activity recovered to 84.7% over the same window and obesity still rose, a
Singapore-grounded argument for putting **diet quality first** rather than "just exercise more"
[@mohNPHS2024].

**The cluster at population scale.** The survey reports the components rather than a bundled figure:
hypertension in about **1 in 3** adults, raised blood lipids in about **1 in 3**, and diabetes in
roughly **1 in 11**, the real-world denominators behind the clustering idea [@mohNPHS2024].

**An ethnic gradient, framed without blame.** Malay and Indian residents carry markedly higher obesity
and diabetes than Chinese residents [@zhang2024bmiSg]. The drivers are genetic, socio-economic, dietary
and environmental rather than moral, and this is what motivates culturally-tailored counselling and the
national **War on Diabetes**; the diabetes detail itself belongs to the *Type 2 Diabetes* chapter
[@mohWarOnDiabetes].

**The policy toolkit to point patients toward:** My Healthy Plate and Nutri-Grade (whose national
roll-out measurably cut the sugar in drinks bought) [@moh2023nutrigradeimpact; @shin2023nutrigrade], the
**Healthier Choice Symbol** on packaged foods [@hpbHealthierChoiceSymbol], and **Healthier SG**, the
enrolled-family-doctor scheme that turns "recognise and refer" into a longitudinal health plan
[@mohHealthierSG]. One dating note: Singapore's Asian BMI cut-offs remain current, but the 2016 obesity
guideline's *treatment* sections predate the GLP-1 era, so read the **cut-offs as current** while
treating its management content as dated [@hpbmoh2016obesityCPG; @lee2023obesitylandscape].

## Is obesity just "eating too much"?

No, and the reasons matter for how we practise. Over-nutrition is a **pattern in an obesogenic
environment**, read at **lower Singaporean cut-offs**, defined less by weight than by **central,
insulin-resistant fat** that pulls a cluster of risks along with it. The response is **lifestyle-first**:
diet quality, activity, sleep, stress and behaviour change, aiming for a modest but powerful 5–10%
loss, with medication and surgery **recognised and referred**, not prescribed here. And because stigma
is itself a metabolic stressor, the framing is not optional: **weight-inclusive, person-first care is
the treatment working, not merely good manners.** From here the cascade continues, into diabetes, and
into the heart and kidneys downstream.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/obesity-metabolic-syndrome.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: Mr Tan and the two number-lines

Mr Tan is 45, a Chinese office worker with a BMI of 28 and a waist of 98 cm. His blood pressure is
138/86, his fasting glucose is nudged up, his HDL is low; he eats a hawker lunch daily (char kway teow,
kopi-C twice a day), manages under 5,000 steps, and sleeps about six hours. Work through the visit:
classify him on the right number-line, decide whether the findings cluster, hunt for the levers across
all five pillars, and notice where reaching for a drug first, or for a blaming word, would be the wrong
move. Your choices branch the case.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/mr-tan-obesity.case.yml"></div>
